_patch_meta.description: Pre-analysis MTI classification for all 29 None-status terms in registry 026 (conscience). Broad-sweep approach per researcher instruction: inclusion is the default, treating this registry as a wide-angle exploration of the full knowing/conscience/moral-awareness semantic field. Only G4862 (sun, Greek preposition 'with') is classified delete — it has no conscience-field contribution. All other 28 terms classified extracted or extracted_thin, grouped into five functional clusters: (1) core conscience vocabulary, (2) moral knowing vocabulary, (3) ignorance as conscience-failure vocabulary, (4) moral judgment/discernment vocabulary, (5) structural/peripheral vocabulary. H3629 kilyah retained in full including sacrificial verses — the ritual use grounds the inner-being-seat theology. Status notes distinguish core from peripheral for analytical clarity.

set.status_note: GROUP 1 — CORE. suneidēsis — conscience. The primary NT conscience term (33 occ, 29 verses). The compound sun + eidēsis (shared knowledge, co-knowing) names the conscience as the faculty that knows alongside the self. Covers: good conscience (Act 23:1; 1 Ti 1:5; Heb 13:18), weak conscience (1 Cor 8:7/10/12), seared conscience (1 Ti 4:2), defiled conscience (Tit 1:15), conscience cleansed by Christ's blood (Heb 9:14), conscience bearing witness (Rom 2:15; Rom 9:1; 2 Cor 1:12), conscience as the ground of obedience (Rom 13:5), and baptism as appeal for a good conscience (1 Pet 3:21). CRL to guilt (073) registered.

GROUP 1 — CORE. suneidō — to be aware of, to be conscious of (verb). Cognate of suneidēsis. 5 occ, 3 verses. Act 5:2 (Sapphira's knowledge of the deception — shared guilty awareness); Act 12:12 (Peter realising where he was); Act 14:6 (they became aware of the plot). CRL to guilt (073) registered.

GROUP 1 — CORE. sunoida — to be conscious of, to share knowledge with oneself. 1 occ, 1 verse. 1 Cor 4:4: 'I am not aware of anything against myself, but I am not thereby acquitted. It is the Lord who judges me.' One of the most analytically significant conscience verses in the corpus — the conscience can be clear while the final verdict belongs to God. Thin data but irreplaceable.

GROUP 1 — CORE. kardiognōstēs — heart-knower (compound: kardia + gnōstēs). 2 occ, 2 verses. Act 1:24 and Act 15:8 — both describe God as the one who knows the hearts of all. The divine conscience-counterpart: God is the ultimate moral knower of persons. Directly relevant to the conscience's structure as co-knowing with God.

GROUP 1 — CORE. kil.yah — kidneys. 31 occ, 26 verses. The OT anatomical seat of conscience and deepest inner scrutiny. Corpus splits: 13 sacrificial/anatomical verses (Exo/Lev ritual use — kidneys as the innermost fat offered to God, grounding the inner-being theology); 13 conscience-seat verses in Psalms and Prophets: Psa 7:9 (God tests minds/hearts), Psa 16:7 (kidneys instruct in the night), Psa 26:2 (test my heart and mind), Psa 73:21 (pricked in heart — kilyah), Psa 139:13 (you formed my inward parts), Pro 23:16 (my inmost being will exult), Jer 11:20 / 17:10 / 20:12 (God searches heart and kidneys/mind). Full verse set retained — the sacrificial use is the theological ground for the inner-being application.

GROUP 2 — MORAL KNOWING. ginōskō — to know, come to know, recognise. 824 occ, 120 verses in export. The primary NT knowing verb. Conscience-relevant subset includes: Mat 6:3 (do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing — moral self-knowledge), Rom 7:7 (through the law I came to know sin), 1 Cor 8:2-3 (knowing as moral epistemology — the one who thinks he knows does not yet know as he ought), 1 Cor 13:12 (now I know in part; then fully known — eschatological conscience resolution), Gal 4:9 (known by God). HIGH_FREQUENCY_ANCHOR applies — 824 occurrences; analysis confined to conscience-relevant verses. Vol limitation flag required.

GROUP 2 — MORAL KNOWING. eidō (perceive/see sense) — to see, perceive, observe. 396 occ, 119 verses. Conscience-relevant verses are a subset — perceptual knowledge that grounds moral awareness. HIGH_FREQUENCY_ANCHOR applies. Vol limitation flag required. Analysis confined to verses with moral-perception content.

GROUP 2 — MORAL KNOWING. eidō (know/understand sense) — to know, possess information, understand. 365 occ, 27 verses. The moral-knowledge register of eidō; conscience-relevant verses include Mat 25:26 (you knew that I reap where I have not sown — moral responsibility grounded in prior knowledge), Mat 27:18 (Pilate knew it was out of envy). HIGH_FREQUENCY_ANCHOR applies. Vol limitation flag required.

GROUP 2 — MORAL KNOWING. epiginōskō — to know fully, recognise, come to understand. 136 occ, 39 verses. The intensive/perfective knowing verb. Key conscience-relevant verses: Mar 2:8 (Jesus perceiving in his spirit their inner questionings), Luk 5:22 (perceived their thoughts), Rom 1:32 (though they know God's righteous decree — moral knowledge that intensifies culpability), 2 Cor 13:5 (examine yourselves — self-knowledge as conscience act), 2 Pe 2:21 (better not to have known the way of righteousness than to turn back). Recognition and moral self-examination are the conscience-relevant registers.

GROUP 2 — MORAL KNOWING. epignōsis — full knowledge, recognition, moral-relational knowing. 25 occ, 20 verses. Directly conscience-relevant: Rom 1:28 (did not see fit to acknowledge God — epignōsis as moral acknowledgment refused), Rom 3:20 (through the law comes knowledge of sin), Heb 10:26 (sinning deliberately after receiving the knowledge of the truth — conscience as the site of wilful post-knowledge sin). Also: Col 3:10 (renewed in knowledge after the image of the creator — restored conscience as image-restoration).

GROUP 2 — MORAL KNOWING. gnōsis — knowledge, understanding. 71 occ, 28 verses. The 1 Cor 8 passage is the central conscience-and-knowledge discourse in the NT: gnōsis (knowledge) and suneidēsis (conscience) are explicitly in dialogue across 1 Cor 8:1-12. 'Knowledge puffs up' (v.1) vs the weak conscience (v.7/10/12) — gnōsis without love/conscience is destructive. Also: 2 Cor 4:6 (light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Christ — moral illumination as conscience-awakening), 2 Cor 10:5 (taking every thought captive to the knowledge of God).

GROUP 3 — IGNORANCE/CONSCIENCE-FAILURE. agnoeō — to be ignorant, not know, not understand. 30 occ, 21 verses. The moral-ignorance register is the conscience's failure mode. Key verses: Act 3:17 (acted in ignorance — moral ignorance as partial exculpation), Act 13:27 (rulers did not recognise him nor understand the prophets — culpable ignorance), Rom 7:15 (I do not understand my own actions — the divided self that cannot account for its own moral behaviour), 1 Cor 14:38 (if anyone is ignorant, let him be ignorant — moral self-deception as its own judgment), 1 Ti 1:13 (I acted ignorantly in unbelief — Paul's pre-conversion conscience state).

GROUP 3 — IGNORANCE/CONSCIENCE-FAILURE. agnoia — ignorance, the state of moral/spiritual unawareness. 15 occ, 4 verses. Act 3:17 (acted in ignorance), Act 17:30 (God overlooked the times of ignorance, but now commands repentance — the transition from pre-conscience ignorance to accountability), Eph 4:18 (darkened in their understanding, alienated from God due to ignorance — hardness of heart as the cause of conscience-darkening), 1 Pet 1:14 (former ignorances — the pre-conversion conscience state named collectively).

GROUP 3 — IGNORANCE/CONSCIENCE-FAILURE. agnōsia — ignorance, especially of God, moral/spiritual blindness. 3 occ, 2 verses. 1 Cor 15:34 (some have no knowledge of God — agnōsia as willful moral-spiritual blindness) and 1 Pet 2:15 (silencing the ignorance of foolish people — the conscience-void state of those who act without moral grounding). The focused anti-God sense gives this term its conscience-relevance.

GROUP 3 — IGNORANCE/CONSCIENCE-FAILURE. agnōstos — unknown. 1 occ, 1 verse. Act 17:23 (altar to the unknown God — the Athenian conscience groping toward a divine reality it cannot name). Thin data; analytically interesting as the conscience-in-absence-of-revelation passage — the pagan conscience sensing what it cannot articulate.

GROUP 4 — MORAL JUDGMENT. gnōmē — judgment, purpose, considered opinion, consent. 33 occ, 8 verses. The conscience as faculty of moral judgment/deliberation. Key verses: 1 Cor 7:25 (I give my judgment as one who by the Lord's mercy is trustworthy — moral deliberation from a formed conscience), 1 Cor 7:40 (in my judgment — apostolic moral assessment), Phm 14 (without your consent — gnōmē as voluntary moral agency, not compelled action), Rev 17:13/17 (of one mind — gnōmē as unified moral orientation, here in the service of the beast vs God's purposes). The organ-of-judgment primary LSJ sense supports its inclusion.

GROUP 4 — MORAL JUDGMENT. gnōrizō — to make known, reveal, disclose. 90 occ, 23 verses. The externalisation of inner knowing — conscience made public or communicated. Key verses: Joh 15:15 (I have made known to you everything — Jesus making the Father's mind known, the divine conscience disclosed), Phil 4:6 (let your requests be made known to God — the conscience-prayer interface), Eph 1:9 (making known the mystery of his will — divine moral purpose disclosed). Conscience as inner knowing that moves toward disclosure.

GROUP 4 — MORAL JUDGMENT. gnōstēs — one who knows, expert in. 5 occ, 1 verse. Act 26:3 (Agrippa is familiar with Jewish customs and controversies — Paul appealing to a conscience formed by knowledge of the law). Thin; the expert-knowledge sense is the conscience formed by deep acquaintance with moral tradition.

GROUP 4 — MORAL JUDGMENT. gnōstos / gnōrimos — known, acquainted with, well-known. 31 occ, 15 verses. The passive dimension of moral knowing — being known, recognised. Key verse: 2 Cor 6:9 (as unknown and yet well known — the apostolic conscience transparent to God while unrecognised by the world). Also: Joh 18:15-16 (the disciple who was known to the high priest — conscience-of-identity in a dangerous context); Act 4:16 (notable miracle known to all).

GROUP 4 — MORAL JUDGMENT. diaginōskō — to determine, decide, examine carefully. 6 occ, 2 verses. Act 23:15 (examine him more carefully — judicial discernment) and Act 24:22 (Felix with accurate knowledge of the Way, deferred judgment). The careful discrimination that conscience requires; thin but the judicial-discernment sense is conscience-adjacent.

GROUP 4 — MORAL JUDGMENT. diagnōrizō — to make known publicly, report. 1 occ, 1 verse. Luk 2:17 (shepherds made known the saying about the child). The public externalisation of received knowledge — the conscience's disclosure function in its most basic form. Very thin.

GROUP 4 — MORAL JUDGMENT / DIVINE KNOWING. proginōskō — to foreknow, to know beforehand. 5 occ, 5 verses. Divine foreknowledge as the supreme form of moral knowing — God's conscience encompasses all persons across all time. Rom 8:29 (foreknew and predestined — the conscience of God toward his people), Rom 11:2 (God has not rejected his people whom he foreknew), 1 Pet 1:20 (Christ foreknown before the foundation of the world). Also 2 Pet 3:17 (knowing this beforehand — human anticipatory moral awareness). The pro- family positions God as the ultimate moral knower who knows before the conscience registers anything.

GROUP 4 — DIVINE KNOWING. prognōsis — foreknowledge. 2 occ, 2 verses. Act 2:23 (delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God), 1 Pet 1:2 (according to the foreknowledge of God the Father). Divine moral foreknowledge as the ground of the atonement — the conscience of God as the frame within which human conscience operates.

GROUP 4 — DIVINE KNOWING. proeidon — to foresee, see beforehand. 2 occ, 2 verses. Act 2:31 (David foresaw the resurrection) and Gal 3:8 (the Scripture foreseeing that God would justify the Gentiles). Prophetic foresight as a form of conscience informed by the Spirit — seeing morally/theologically before the event.

GROUP 4 — MORAL JUDGMENT. sungnōmē — concession, forbearance, leniency of judgment. 1 occ, 1 verse. 1 Cor 7:6 (as a concession, not a command). The conscience exercising lenient judgment rather than absolute rule — moral flexibility grounded in pastoral wisdom. Very thin data but the term's LSJ range (fellow-feeling, pardon) is conscience-adjacent.

GROUP 5 — STRUCTURAL/PERIPHERAL. anaginōskō — to read, read aloud. 83 occ, 29 verses. The public reading of Scripture as the external standard against which conscience is calibrated. Key conscience-relevant verses: 'Have you not read...?' (Mat 12:3/5; 19:4 etc — Jesus invoking the read text as the moral norm), 2 Cor 3:2 (you are our letter written on our hearts, to be known and read by all — the conscience as a text), 2 Cor 3:15 (when Moses is read a veil lies over their hearts — the hardened conscience that cannot receive the written standard), Rev 1:3 (blessed is the one who reads aloud and keeps — reading as moral formation). The law-reading / Scripture-reading dimension is the external formation of the inner conscience.

GROUP 5 — STRUCTURAL/PERIPHERAL. anagnōrizō — to recognise, make known again. 2 occ, 1 verse. Act 7:13 (Joseph made himself known to his brothers — self-disclosure after concealment). The re-recognition motif: conscience as the faculty that re-knows what was formerly hidden or denied. Thin.

GROUP 5 — STRUCTURAL/PERIPHERAL. ke.li — vessel, article, utensil, implement. 325 occ, 225 verses. The primary conscience-relevant register is the vessel/container metaphor for the person as the bearer of inner moral content — 1 Pet 3:7 ('the weaker vessel') and the Temple vessel theology (vessels consecrated/clean vs defiled). The majority of the corpus is literal (weapons, bags, jewelry) but the vessel-as-person metaphor runs through both testaments. HIGH_FREQUENCY_ANCHOR applies — analysis focused on the metaphorical/theological vessel register. Vol limitation flag required. Note: this is the broadest-sweep inclusion in this registry and should be treated with particular caution in synthesis.

Greek preposition σύν ('with'). 278 occurrences. No conscience-field contribution regardless of analytical approach. Confirmed extraction artefact — same issue identified in registry 097 (joy). Delete.

record.description: G1097 ginōskō has 824 occurrences and 120 verses in export. Only a conscience-relevant subset has been analysed. Full corpus is far beyond Session B scope. Phase 2 independent research required before synthesis-level conclusions drawn from this term's full range.
G1492H eidō (see/perceive) has 396 occurrences and 119 verses in export. Only conscience-morally-relevant subset analysed. Phase 2 required.
G1492I eidō (know/understand) has 365 occurrences and 27 verses in export. Conscience-morally-relevant subset only. Phase 2 required.

H3627 ke.li has 325 occurrences and 225 verses in export. Classified extracted under broad-sweep approach for vessel/container metaphor relevance. The majority of the corpus is literal (weapons, bags, utensils) — not conscience vocabulary. Analysis must be confined strictly to the vessel-as-person / consecrated-vessel theological register. Phase 2 review required before any synthesis-level use of this term.

G1921 epiginōskō has 136 occurrences and 39 verses in export. Conscience-relevant subset (recognition, moral self-examination, spiritual perception) is a minority of the full corpus. Phase 2 required for full assessment.

operations.note: BROAD-SWEEP REGISTRY: conscience (026) has been classified under a researcher-directed broad-sweep approach — inclusion as default, treating the registry as a wide-angle exploration of the full knowing/conscience/moral-awareness semantic field. 28 of 29 terms extracted or extracted_thin; only G4862 (preposition) deleted. Session B analysis must handle the resulting corpus with explicit acknowledgment of the five functional groups and their varying proximity to the conscience core. The HIGH_FREQUENCY_ANCHOR terms (G1097, G1492H, G1492I, H3627) require particular analytical caution — synthesis-level conclusions must not be drawn from their full verse corpora.

_patch_summary.note_on_duplicate: OP-018 is a placeholder noting G1108 was already classified in OP-011; no DB action needed
